blocks.text: Steven Lowe
Vice President, Finance
{{fact:f:E-2009-002.client}}
Re: Growth Strategy for Key Group
Dear Mr. Lowe:

This letter sets the terms on which we will help Key Group decide where its growth is going to come from over the next several years and what it will take to capture it. It records the work, the people who will do it, the fee, and the conditions we work under. Please read it against your own memory of our conversations. If any line here does not match what you understood, telephone me before you sign, so we settle it at the outset. It is written to be read alongside your own recollection of our recent conversations, and it is deliberately specific about what we will and will not do, because an engagement of this kind depends on both sides holding the same understanding of scope from the first day. Once you countersign, this describes the scope, and we will hold to it.
The Work

Our task is to answer a single question and answer it well: where should Key Group look for its next several years of growth, and what will it cost to get there. Within that, the engagement does four things. We will read the current position, establishing where the company earns its money today by segment and by customer, and where that base is growing or thinning. We will size the market, measuring the segments the company serves and the near ones it does not, and marking where demand is moving. We will frame the growth options, covering deeper penetration of the core, extension into adjacent segments, and geographic extension, each carrying an honest account of what it would require and what it would return. And we will prioritize, ranking the options on return and on what the company can realistically execute, and laying out a plan for the ones worth pursuing.
What This Engagement Is Not

The boundaries are worth stating plainly, so that none of them is left to inference. This is not a valuation, and we take no view on the price of any transaction. We do not raise capital or advise on financing. We do not provide legal or tax advice; where a question turns on either, we will say so in writing and refer you to your own counsel. Our work ends at a recommendation and a plan you can act on. We do not implement it for you, though we are glad to be asked separately if you want that help later.
How We Will Work

We build from your own numbers, not from a template, and we document as we go, so any figure that reaches a deliverable can be tied back to a source your finance staff already recognize. We keep findings separate from recommendations and label each for what it is, because the two carry different weight. And we bring interim results to you as they firm up, so nothing in the final material arrives as a surprise.
Who Will Do It

Brenda Rodriguez, Senior Consultant, will lead the analysis day to day and will be your team's first point of contact on any matter of substance. Daniel Leach, Research Associate, will run the market sizing and the data collection, and your finance staff will hear from him most often. I read every deliverable before it leaves this office. For scheduling and data requests, our contact on your side will be you, Steven Lowe, unless you direct us to someone else.
Fee and Start Date

Our fee to {{fact:f:E-2009-002.client}} for the work described above is {{fact:f:E-2009-002.fee}}, fixed for that scope. We will begin on {{fact:f:E-2009-002.start}}, and we plan on approximately seven months from that date to the final material. If you ask us to widen the scope, we will price the addition in writing and will not begin it until you have agreed the price.
Invoicing

We will invoice the fixed fee in equal monthly installments across the term, each payable within thirty days of its date. Out-of-pocket expenses, principally travel to your sites, are billed at cost and itemized on the invoice. We do not mark expenses up.
Term and Termination

The engagement runs from the start date set out above until we deliver the final material, unless either of us ends it sooner. Either party may terminate on fifteen days' written notice, for any reason or none. On termination you will owe the fee for work performed to the effective date, prorated against the fixed fee, together with expenses incurred, and we will hand over the work product in whatever state it has then reached.
Confidentiality

We will treat the information you give us as confidential, use it only for this engagement, and disclose it outside our firm only as you direct in writing or as law requires. Growth work of this kind touches plans you have not yet made public, and we will handle it with that in mind. We ask the same of you as to our methods, our workpapers, and the terms of this fee.
Our Liability

Our total liability arising out of this engagement, on any theory, is limited to the fees actually paid to us under it. Neither party will be liable to the other for indirect, incidental, or consequential loss, including lost profits, however it arises. This limit does not reach our own fraud or willful misconduct. Our deliverables are prepared for your internal use; if you intend to give one to a third party who may rely on it, raise it with us first so that we can address the reliance in writing.
Governing Law

This letter is governed by the law of the state in which our principal office sits, without regard to its conflict-of-laws rules. Any dispute we cannot resolve between us will go to mediation before either party commences litigation.

If these terms are acceptable, please sign both copies of this letter and return one to us; the other is yours to keep. We are glad to be doing this work for Key Group, and we will be ready to begin on the date set out above.
Sincerely,
To: Daniel Leach
From: Brenda Rodriguez
Subject: Kickoff, growth strategy for Key Group

We begin on {{fact:f:E-2009-002.start}}. The question {{fact:f:E-2009-002.client}} has put to us is narrow, and I want us to keep it that way: where does Key Group's growth come from over the next several years, and what does it cost to get there. We owe them a read of the current position, a sized view of the market, a short list of growth options ranked on return and on what the company can actually execute, and a plan for the ones worth doing. Everything below serves that and nothing else. Our contact on the client side is Steven Lowe, VP Finance; requests to their staff go through his office so nobody is asked for the same file twice.

A word on how the work breaks up. It runs roughly seven months, and I read that as three parts: a first stretch building the current position and the market sizing, a middle stretch framing and testing the options, and a final stretch on the plan and the material we hand over. We are at the front of the first part. I want the base right before we move on it, because every option we frame later is only as good as the sizing and the current-state work underneath it. That is where our time goes now.
The work, in the order I expect us to do it

items: Current position. I will build the picture of where Key Group earns today, by segment and by customer, and where the base is growing or thinning. Everything else stands on this, so it goes first and I will not rush it.

Market sizing. Daniel Leach will size the segments Key Group serves and the near ones it does not, from published sources and the client's own figures, and mark where demand is moving. Source every number; we will be asked for them.

Growth options. Once the first two are solid, we frame the options: deeper penetration of the core, adjacent segments, and geographic extension. Each option arrives with what it takes and what it returns, or it does not go on the list. An option we cannot support stays off.
Prioritization and plan. Last, we rank the options that survive and sequence the ones worth pursuing. This depends on the three above and must not run ahead of them.

blocks.text: Getting started this week

I will send the consolidated data request to Steven Lowe and get the interviews with the segment leads on calendars. Daniel Leach, start the market sources in parallel so we are not sitting idle waiting on the client's data. Bring me your open questions before we meet the client, not after. And keep a running log of what we have asked for and what has come back, so we can show the client our working the first time a number is questioned.

There is one assumption I do not want us to carry unexamined: that the segment reporting the client keeps lines up with how the market actually divides. If it does not, the sizing work changes shape and some of the current-position cuts will not tie, so I want it surfaced in the first two weeks while we can still act on it. Flag it the moment you see the segment definitions, and do not paper over a mismatch to keep the schedule; a clean base is worth more to this engagement than an early one.
Steven Lowe, with Daniel Leach copied:

A short status and one request. We have the first data pull and the segment revenue history, and I have started laying out where {{fact:f:E-2009-002.client}} earns today, segment by segment. Daniel Leach has the market sources going in parallel, so the sizing and the current-position work are moving together instead of one waiting on the other.

The gap we have is the customer-level detail underneath the two largest segments. We can work from the segment totals for now, but the customer cut is what tells us whether the growth in those segments is broad across the base or sitting in a handful of accounts, and that difference changes the whole read. We do not need it broken out by month, only by account for the current year and the two before it. If your finance team can send that by the end of next week, we stay on plan.

Nothing else is needed from you today. The interviews with the segment leads at Key Group are the next thing on our side, and I will send you the proposed times tomorrow so your assistants can hold the slots.
Brenda Rodriguez
Steven Lowe, copying Daniel Leach:

Thanks for the quick turn on the segment file over the weekend. Daniel Leach has loaded it, and the customer-level detail is there for the largest segment, though the second one still comes through at the segment total only. We can start on the first and come back to the second, so this does not hold us up. I have flagged in our notes which segment is running on totals, so nothing downstream mistakes it for account-level detail.

The one open item is scheduling. The segment leads at Key Group have given us three of the four interview slots. The outstanding one is the head of the industrial line, who I understand is traveling until the following week. If someone on your side can help us land thirty minutes with him, that closes the set. The current-position read for {{fact:f:E-2009-002.client}} is firmer once we have heard from all four, so I would like to close that interview before we go much further.
That is the only thing I need from you right now. I will keep this thread updated as the interviews land and the sizing fills in.
Brenda Rodriguez
Growth Strategy for {{fact:f:E-2009-002.client}}

Prepared by the engagement team at Key Group's request
Brenda Rodriguez, Senior Consultant, and Daniel Leach, Research Associate

blocks.text: What We Are Hearing So Far

items: Growth in the two largest segments looks concentrated in a handful of accounts, not spread across the base
The nearest adjacent segments are larger than the core, but more contested
Geographic extension comes up repeatedly in the interviews, without a clear read yet on what it would cost
These are early observations from three of four interviews, offered as observations and not yet as findings

items: Current-position picture built to the segment level; customer detail in hand for the largest segment
Market sizing under way, roughly half complete
Three of the four segment-lead interviews done
No growth options are on the table yet, and none should be read into this briefing

blocks.text: What Comes Next

items: Close the last interview and finish the market sizing
Frame three or four growth options, with their requirements and returns, for discussion and not for decision
Bring the current-position read and the option set to the next working session
Questions to Brenda Rodriguez; data questions direct to Daniel Leach

blocks.text: Working Session: Current Position and Growth Options
Minutes recorded by Daniel Leach. Subject: {{fact:f:E-2009-002.client}}, working session with the client on the growth strategy. The notes below follow the order of discussion.
Attendees

items: Brenda Rodriguez, Senior Consultant
Daniel Leach, Research Associate
Steven Lowe, Vice President, Finance, Key Group

blocks.text: Current Position

Brenda Rodriguez presented the current-position analysis as it now stands. The finding she put forward is that growth in the two largest segments is concentrated: in each, a small number of accounts account for the majority of the increase over the period, while the wider base is close to flat. She was careful to mark this as a measurement from the segment and customer files, not yet a conclusion about why, and she noted that the second-largest segment still comes through at the segment total for two months of the period, which she is reconciling before she will call the number firm. Steven Lowe said the concentration matched his own sense of the largest segment, and he offered two account names we had not yet tied to the pattern. I recorded both for follow-up.
Market Sizing

I walked the table through the market sizing as far as it has gone. The segments Key Group serves are, in total, smaller than the adjacent segments the company does not serve today, and demand in those adjacent segments is growing faster than in the core. I noted two limits on the figures. First, the published sources disagree on the size of one adjacent segment by a wide margin, and I have used the lower of the two pending a third source. Second, the sizing is roughly half complete and the numbers should be read as provisional. Brenda Rodriguez asked that the range, not a single point, carry into the option work until the third source is in, and it was agreed.
Growth Options, in Outline

We reviewed the growth options in outline, without ranking them. Three are on the working list: deeper penetration of the concentrated accounts in the core, extension into the nearest adjacent segment, and geographic extension of the existing lines. Steven Lowe asked which of the three the analysis currently favors. Brenda Rodriguez declined to rank them yet, on the ground that the market sizing is not closed and a ranking made now would harden into a recommendation before the evidence supports one. It was agreed that the options would be framed with their requirements and returns at the next session, and ranked only after.
Open Items

Two items remain open, and both bear on the option work. The first is the reconciliation of the two months in the second-largest segment, which Brenda Rodriguez holds. The second is the third market source for the disputed adjacent segment, which I hold. Neither should be treated as settled in the interim.
Action Items

rows: Reconcile the two open months in the second-largest segment and confirm the concentration finding
Obtain a third source for the disputed adjacent segment and carry the range into the sizing
Provide the two additional account names tied to the concentration pattern
Frame the three growth options with their requirements and returns, unranked
